coredata.dc:title: Harnessing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for enhancing the fluorescence and conductivity of the starch-based film
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chenxi
Wen, Mengying
Mai, Shihua
Ma, Yue
Duan, Qingfei
Bao, Xianyang
Zou, Wei
Liu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The flexible film is widely applied in the modern electronic industry, whilst it is still challenging to use biopolymer substrates (e.g., starch) to prepare flexible film well-performed in conductivity and fluorescence. In the study, a novel conductive, fluorescent, and flexible biopolymer film was prepared via a cost-effective method by fabricating the nitrogen-doped oxide-reduced graphene quantum dots (N-rGO-QDs) into the thermoplastic starch (TPS) substrate. TPS/N-rGO-QDs film with 10 wt% N-rGO-QDs showed the desirable lowest resistivity (0.082 Ω·m), acceptable light transmittance (60–80 %), and durable fluorescence intensity (9000 CPS). The results reveal a novel starch-based multifunctional film with satisfactory electrical and fluorescent performances, which is hypothesized potential to be applied in some frontier domains, like human wearable devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch-based film
N-rGO-QDs
Fluorescence
Conductivity
Flexibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-rGO-QDs and TPS/N-rGO-QDs film
Morphology and optical properties of TPS/N-rGO-QDs films
Fluorescence and conductivity of TPS/N-rGO-QDs film
The application of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film
Reliability testing of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and technology plan project of Guangzhou

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge research funds Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32272340 , 22178124 ), the 111 project ( B17018 ), Science and technology plan project of Guangzhou ( 202102080150 ). National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of South China University of China.

The authors would like to acknowledge research funds Natural Science Foundation of China (32272340, 22178124), the 111 project (B17018), Science and technology plan project of Guangzhou (202102080150). National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of South China University of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the preparation of N-rGO-QDs and the TPS/N-rGO-QDs film.
Gelatinization endotherms (A) and enthalpy (B) of native corn starch and the mixtures with different contents of N-rGO-QDs.
XRD (A) and FT-IR (B) diagrams of TPS and TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films with different N-rGO-QDs contents.
AFM (A-E) of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composites films with 0 wt%, 1.0 wt%, 3.0 wt%, 5.0 wt% and 10.0 wt% N-rGO-QDs, respectively.
The transparency (A) and light transmittance (B) of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film.
The strain-tensile stress curve (A), Young's modulus (left) and tensile stress (right) (B), elongation (C), and folding times (D) of TPS and TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films (n = 7).

UV–vis absorption and image of GQDs and N-rGO-QDs(A); cyclic voltammograms (B) and EIS curves (C) of Nafion/N-rGO-QDs/GCE; fluorescence spectrum of different concentrations of N-rGO-QDs under 350 nm excitation (D).

Photographs of composite films were taken in daylight (left) and UV lamp (right) (A), and UV absorption of the N-rGO-QDs and composite films (B). The fluorescence intensity of N-rGO-QDs, TPS and composite films (C), and fluorescence stability of composite films within 20 days(D). The film diameter is 9 cm and the thickness is ∼22 μm.

Plots of resistance change for TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films at bending angle of 30°(A) and 90°(B). The inset images of Fig. 9. A-B are digital photographs and schematic illustrations. The relative resistance changes (C) and current (D) of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films for real-time monitoring finger bent with different angles.
Resistance and resistivity of N-rGO-QDs/TPS composite films (n = 7).

head.ce:title: Harnessing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for enhancing the fluorescence and conductivity of the starch-based film

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The flexible film is widely applied in the modern electronic industry, whilst it is still challenging to use biopolymer substrates (e.g., starch) to prepare flexible film well-performed in conductivity and fluorescence. In the study, a novel conductive, fluorescent, and flexible biopolymer film was prepared via a cost-effective method by fabricating the nitrogen-doped oxide-reduced graphene quantum dots (N-rGO-QDs) into the thermoplastic starch (TPS) substrate. TPS/N-rGO-QDs film with 10 wt% N-rGO-QDs showed the desirable lowest resistivity (0.082 Ω·m), acceptable light transmittance (60–80 %), and durable fluorescence intensity (9000 CPS). The results reveal a novel starch-based multifunctional film with satisfactory electrical and fluorescent performances, which is hypothesized potential to be applied in some frontier domains, like human wearable devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible conductive or fluorescent film is widely applied in modern electronic signal sensors or optoelectronic device (Han et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2016). Whilst it is usually produced from the lightweight, chemical stable and transparent polyimide (Jiang et al., 2019), polycarbonate (Liu et al., 2021), polyethylene terephthalate (Song et al., 2018) or naphthalate (Chen et al., 2019), all these traditional petrochemical materials are not easy to be handled and degraded, and the discarding and accumulation of these materials pose a potential threat to human health and environment. In this regard, a use of natural polymers sourced from biomass for fabricating cheap, biodegradable film and a development of “green” and facile fabrication techniques are highly demanded (Moro et al., 2019).

Starch with the advantages of low cost, good film-forming ability, renewability, biological performance and degradability, have attracted extensive attentions and been applied to develop flexible conductive materials with ionic liquid (IL) and conductive polymer (Ccorahua et al., 2019; Han et al., 2018; Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2021; Zhang et al., 2017). Nevertheless, the earlier starch-based conductive materials were often defective as an electrical component due to a significant high or unstable resistance (Zamora-Sequeira et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). Recently, Liu et al. (2020) successfully developed a starch-based hydrogel film using with calcium chloride to enhance conductivity, but such film presented unstable resistance that is highly variable depending on water content. To enhance the conductivity of starch films, starch substrate could also be composited with a series of novel conductive carbon-based additives, such as carbon fiber (Wang et al., 2016), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) (Domene-López et al., 2019) to prepare thin conductive film as a wearable device. However, due to the poor compatibility with starch, these carbon-based additives presented an uneven distribution in the matrix and usually caused unstable conductive and poor light transmittance. Fortunately, comparing with semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have long received considerable attention for their remarkable electronic, good water solubility, low-toxicity and physicochemical properties (Xu et al., 2021). Despite starch as substrate was combined with GQDs to develop functional films (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017), it presented an unfavorable high resistance (Chen et al., 2019). To address this challenge, the electron transfer of GQDs was attempted to enhance via nitrogen doping, while the graphene structure of N-GQDs experienced reduction reaction in presence of VC. The novelty and advantages of N-rGO-QDs preparation is using green reducing agent, which is widely employed as a food additive, and the integration of nitrogen doping and reduction processes by one-pot solvothermal method greatly improved the electron transport efficiency of GQDs in this system. Moreover, starch is firstly quite advantageous as a widely present bio-polymers substrate convenient to obtain and process into film materials. The entire process is so mild to allow effective incorporation of varied functional components such as mineral salts, GQDs and their derivatives through a simple physical stirring. Also, the abundant -OH groups contained in starch molecules provide many ideal chemical sites to be interacted with the functional components to help achieve better performances (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017). Moreover, starch substrate often shows a flexible Young's modulus ranging from 20 to 800 MPa, making the film materials more physically durable and stretchable when applied to wearable electrical devices (Ccorahua et al., 2019). In addition, starch, being a well-known bio-polymer substrate, is meant to contribute additional benefits (e.g., bio-degradable, renewable) to the electrical composite film in practice. Despite all advantages above, however, it is still unclear of the impacts of the starch substrate on some key parameters of the electrical films, i.e., fluorescence and conductivity. To address the knowledge gaps, the novel electrical composite film was prepared by incorporating N-rGO-QDs into the starch substrate, expected to reveal its influences on the crucial fluorescence and conductivity.

In this work, a new strategy is applied to develop the intelligent starch-based film with high conductivity and fluorescence using the hydrophilic N-rGO-QDs as nano-fillers. The composite film was characterized through the combined techniques including differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), followed by a detailed analysis of electrocatalytic efficiency, fluorescent and conductive stability. It is hypothesized that the conductivity and fluorescence performance of starch-based film were simultaneously enhanced by adding hydrophilic and well dispersed N-rGO-QDs, and the films could be practically employed as a wearable bending sensor to trace the small-scale human movements directly. The research novelty lies in that the starch-based film was endowed with multi-functions by compositing the multifunctional N-rGO-QDs, hence showing excellent fluorescence and conductivity. It hopefully suggests a new generation of flexible electronic components applied in wearable devices, quantum light-emitting diodes (QLEDs), and convertors in future.

Citric acid (CA, weight-averaged molecular weight 192.13), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), urea, L-(þ)-Ascorbic acid (vitamin C, VC), and glycerol (99.5 % purity) were purchased as analytical-grade reagents from Shanghai National Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Corn starch with the amylose content of 24 % and moisture content of 13.5 %, respectively, was obtained from China Oil and Foodstuffs Corporation. Water used in the experiment was deionized (18.25 MΩ cm). Nafion (≥98.0 %) was obtained from Sigma–Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

N-rGO-QDs were prepared according to the one-pot solvent thermal method (Ozyurt et al., 2021). Briefly, 2.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were put into a 100 mL beaker and heated up to 200 °C using a heating mantle. After 30 min the obtained black liquid, containing N-GQDs, was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave (150 mL) with 200 mL aqueous solution of VC (50 mg/mL) and NaOH (0.4 mg/mL) in Teflon lining at 100 °C for 10 h. The mass ratio of N-GQDs and VC was 1:4. After that, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and followed by 1 h ultrasonication (40 kHz, 600 W), and dried at room temperature (25 °C). The final product of N-rGO-QDs is 3.0 g, and the yield of N-rGO-QDs is about 23.1 %. Before the fluorescence test, 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution was added into N-rGO-QDs with vigorous stirring. Unused N-rGO-QDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C in dark.

TPS/N-rGO-QDs films were prepared using a casting method. 5.0 g native corn starch, 0.5 g glycerol and proper amount of N-rGO-QDs (formula of Table S1) were mixed with distilled water to prepare 100 mL solution, and heated at 90 °C with continuous stirring for 1 h. Subsequently, the pre-polymerization mixture of TPS/N-rGO-QDs was spread into a polystyrene Petri dish and dried in an oven at 38 °C for 8 h.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Phenom Desktop, Phenom-World, the Netherlands), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi Japan) and atomic force microscope (AFM, EC-AFM, Oxford U.S.A.) were used to observe the morphology of N-rGO-QDs and TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films. The height of the N-rGO-QDs was observed using a silicon wafer as the sample carrier. The height and distribution of N-rGO-QDs in the composite films were observed with epoxy resin coating.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Aquinox 55, Germany) was collected in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. Before measurement, the films were cut into pieces with a size of 20 × 20 mm.

13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy (NMR) measurements were taken using an AVANCE III HD 600 (Bruker, Switzerland). 300 mg of GQDs and 300 mg of N-rGO-QDs were added into D2O were prepared for proton NMR analysis.
X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Germany) XRD analyses were performed using a Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV in the scan range of 2θ from 5 to 50°.
Raman spectra were measured at room temperature using a Raman microscope (Renishaw inVia™ Qontor, England) with 785 nm laser excitation.
The mean particle size index of the N-rGO-QDs was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS, ZETA-AIZER, Malvern UK) with a laser particle analyzer.

UV spectrum of GQDs, N-GQDs, N-rGO-QDs and composite films were measured by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-2550 spectrometer, Shimadzu, Japan). All inset figures were shot using Nikon digital sight DS-Fil camera.
The surface roughness of the obtained film was measured by an Up Dual-mode 3D optical profiler (Rtec Instrument, USA).

An electrochemical workstation (Instrument Model: CHI760E, China) was applied to measure electrochemical behaviors. Initially, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished on a mirror surface with 0.05 μm alumina slurry, and washed fully with absolute ethanol and ultrapure water, and then ultrasonically shaken for 1 min to remove the polishing powder from the surface of GCE. After it, the GCE was coated with 1.0 mg N-rGO-QDs suspension solution and then 5.0 μL Nafion (2.0 mg/L) solution was dropped on the surface. Subsequently the Nafion-N-rGO-QDs/GCE was slightly washed with PBS solution (50.0 M) to remove the redundant Nafion.

The electrochemical behavior of N-rGO-QDs was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement. CV was performed in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 0.1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] over the voltage range between −0.3 V and + 0.5 V with a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test of N-rGO-QDs was conducted from 100 mHz to 100 kHz with an alternating current (AC) amplitude of 10 mV. The samples were enforced to reach water equilibrium under 33, 57, and 75 % of humid environment respectively prior to a further reliability test.

Tensile testing of the film was conducted with an ASTM D5938 standard using Instron tensile apparatus (5565, USA). The testing section of the dumbbell-shaped bar (35 mm in length, 2 mm in width) was positioned between the tensile grips with a 25 mm initial distance. The extension rate was kept at 10 mm/min. The tensile strength and elongation at break were evaluated by the 0.1 N load force of the Instron software. Before measurements, all the samples were balanced at a 75 % RH, and the results given were mean values of seven specimens.

Film folding measurements were performed by a MIT bending strength tester (ME/BK-01, HUYGEN, England) with a bending angle of 135°. Samples were cut in a uniform size of 15 × 150 mm. Measurements were performed in triplicate.

The fluorescence intensity of N-rGO-QDs and TPS/N-rGO-QDs films was tested using a fluorescence spectrometer (FLUOROMAX-4, HoRiBA Scientific, U.S.A.) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm within a recording emission range of 370–680 nm. The excitation and emission wavelength slit widths were set at 2 nm and 5 nm, respectively, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was 800 eV. Typically, a given concentration of N-rGO-QDs was put into a quartz cell and then tested in triplicate.

The composite films were cut into pieces with a size of 1 cm (width) × 5 cm (length) × ~0.30 mm (thickness), and then tested at room condition, with a temperature of 25 °C and a relative humidity (RH) of ~57 %. Besides, the film devices were attached on the fingers to trace the human actions. Resistance (R or R
0 , Ω) and square resistance (Rs, Ω/sq) were measured by a digital multimeter (Keithley DMM 7510, Tektronix Company, USA) with open-circuit current. ρ = Rs·d·w/L was used to evaluate resistivity, where Rs, d, L and w are square resistance, width, length and film thickness, respectively (d = L = 10 mm in Rs test). ∆R/R
0 = (R − R
0
)/R
0 was used to calculate the relative change rate. R
0 and R were the original resistance and resistance under bending, respectively (Tie et al., 2021). The resistance and electrical current were meanwhile recorded in detail to confirm the sensitivity of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film when being bent underwater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-rGO-QDs and TPS/N-rGO-QDs film
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic diagram presenting a preparation of N-rGO-QDs is shown in Fig. 1 . N-rGO-QDs, the key functional additive, were successfully prepared using the two consecutive steps: a). Amidated citric acid as the precursor was mixed with urea to construct the graphitic structure of N-GQDs via dehydration and deamination; b). Subsequently, N-GQDs were derived to the N-rGO-QDs by using a two-step nucleophilic reduction reaction with l-ascorbic acid.

After the chemical conversion from the precursors to N-rGO-QDs, a systematic structure characterization was carried out. In XRD spectra (Fig. S1A), there is a strong peak appeared at around 26.6° for GQDs, N-GQDs and N-rGO-QDs, indicating that the graphene structure was formed during the hydrothermal reaction. Such graphene structure was also confirmed by Raman spectra (Fig. S1B) with an appearance of the disordered D-band (~ 1336 cm−1) and crystalline G-band (~ 1581 cm−1), which were caused by graphite edges and originated from the in-phase vibration of graphite lattice, respectively (Bayat & Saievar, 2017; Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). Since the redox reactive conversion of N-GQDs into N-rGO-QDs induced a clear increase of D-band intensity, D/G ratio was often as an effective parameter to reflect the conversional effects (Chen, Dai, et al., 2019). In the study GQDs, N-GQDs and N-rGO-QDs were characterized to show an obvious increase of D/G ratio at 0.85, 1.25 and 1.26 respectively, suggesting N-GQDs were at least partially converted into N-rGO-QDs. The morphology of the N-rGO-QDs at nanoscale was observed through TEM (Fig. S1D), AFM (Fig. S1E) and DLS (Fig. S2). N-rGO-QDs was of characterized of a plan view size (5.0 × 35.0 nm) plus its topographic height at 0.5–2.0 nm, around three times thick as the single-layer rGO (0.335–0.5 nm) reported elsewhere (Lee et al., 2013; Si & Samulski, 2008). It is thus inferred that a triple-layers structure might be formed for N-rGO-QDs.

FT-IR spectrophotometer was used to analyze the stretching vibrations from a series of functional groups like CC (1670 cm−1), CN (1572 cm−1), CN (1347 cm−1), NH (1175 cm−1, 3242 cm−1). As shown in Fig. S1C, the stretching vibrations of CN, CN, NH indicated that the nitrogen was successfully doped into N-GQDs or N-rGO-QDs as expected (Lee et al., 2013; Li et al., 2018). In addition, the stretching vibration from the included hydrophilic groups -OH (3400 cm−1) and –NH2 (1175 cm−1, 3242 cm−1), suggested that N-rGO-QDs are highly hydrophilic. Furthermore,13C NMR spectra were employed and as shown in Fig. S3 signals from 168 ppm were clearly observed, which is the typical peak of C=N/C=O structure, indicating that presence of pyrrole-N and pyridone-N. It corresponded with the result of FT-IR. Such hydrophilic property was also confirmed by zeta potential analysis with a value of −51.6 mV (absolute value>30), indicating that a desirable dispersion in water was achieved (Si & Samulski, 2008).

Due to the good dispersity and hydrophilic property, it is well expected that N-rGO-QDs could be compatible with the hydrophilic starch molecules. The effect of N-rGO-QDs on the thermal behavior of starch was studied using DSC and the thermogram, as shown in Fig. 2 . It is seen that, a native gelatinization endotherm appeared at the temperature range from about 64.5 °C to 80 °C (Xu et al., 2022). With addition of N-rGO-QDs, the starch mixtures exhibited a similar endotherm with a higher peak temperature (Tp) and enthalpy (△H). It indicated the presence of N-rGO-QDs did not change the essential property of gelatinization, but partially delayed the process. Since N-rGO-QDs are organic chemical compounds derived from citric acid with many hydroxyls groups, it is expected that they partially interacted with starch and water molecules through hydrogen bonding to cause the change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of TPS/N-rGO-QDs films
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films were successfully prepared, as shown in Fig. 3A of XRD diagrams of the mixed signals from TPS and N-rGO-QDs. The typical peaks appeared at 19.78°, 14.73° and 22.49°, are associated with the A- and V-type crystalline of starch, while the peak of 26.98° is attributed to the ordered crystal structure of N-rGO-QDs (Chen et al., 2019; Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017). Such mixed signals were also confirmed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3B). In addition, following the increase of N-rGO-QDs content, it was seen the gradually higher FTIR spectra signals occurred at ∼1572 cm−1 (CN) and 3200–3500 cm−1 (NH) respectively, suggesting N-rGO-QDs should be successfully doped into starch substrate. Also, the spectra signal appeared at 3300 cm−1 might be partially attributed to the typical hydroxyl groups stretching vibration, which means more hydroxyl groups were present in the composite films.

The morphology of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films were exhibited in Fig. 4 and Fig. S4 with SEM, TEM and AFM observations. It is seen that, as N-rGO-QDs content was low than 3.0 wt%, the composite films presented a relatively uniform distribution of the nanoparticles including some N-rGO-QDs agglomeration and showed quite smooth appearance. Once N-rGO-QDs was increased to 10.0 wt%, the morphology of the composite film showed rougher surface due to a higher concentration of N-rGO-QDs, but no obvious phase separation was found through the cross-section observation (Fig. S4a-e). In fact, it is expected that a good compatibility between starch and N-rGO-QDs is presented in the composite film, which is agreed with the results of DSC and FT-IR. Following a further increase of N-rGO-QDs content, it was seen of a horizontal dimensional diameter is high than 50 nm for the aggregated N-rGO-QDs nanoparticles (Fig. S4J). It is worthy to note, the unique triple-layered graphene structure of N-rGO-QDs were well kept in the film since the particle height of the N-rGO-QDs was still at around 1.0–2.0 nm, which is similar as the original nanoparticles (Fig. 4A-E) with the height of single-layer rGO is 0.335–0.500 nm (Si & Samulski, 2008). It also indicated that no significant vertical self-stacking of N-rGO-QDs occurred, which is expected from that the strong hydrogen interaction between starch and N-rGO-QDs hindered the vertical movement of N-rGO-QDs (Chen, Dai, et al., 2019; Gong et al., 2021).

The 3D morphologies of the composite films surface topographies were shown in Fig. S5 and the roughness of the composite films was presented in Table S3. RMS roughness (Sq) is the arithmetic mean of the absolute value of the contour offset within the sampling length, calculated by Gwyddion software. In addition, the mean roughness (Sa) and surface area are also roughness indicators. In Table S3, it is seen that the roughness of the surface, Sq and Sa, increased with increasing N-rGO-QDs content. TPS has the lowest surface roughness (Sq = 4.95 μm, Sa =2.7 μm, and surface area = 2.46 mm2), while the composite film with 10.0 wt% content of N-rGO-QDs has the highest surface roughness (Sq = 13. 57 μm, Sa =7.62 μm, and surface area = 2.91 mm2).

The optical transparency of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films was inspected via mere visual observation and UV–vis spectrophotometer characterization. It is seen that the color of the composite film gradually become darker due to the increased N-rGO-QDs content when compared with pure TPS.

To further analyze the light transmittance in presence of N-rGO-QDs, the key concept “quantum size effect” must be illustrated well in advance. It refers to a well-known phenomenon that quasi-continuous electron near to Fermi level would become discrete once the QDs particle size smaller within a certain range, where QDs are often seen a stronger fluorescence intensity and a typical blueshift of UV spectra. In contrast, following an up to 10 wt% addition of N-rGO-QDs aggregates of a larger size (Figs. 4 & S4), it was instead to see a weakened fluorescence intensity together with a typical redshift of UV spectra (Fig. 5B), suggesting QDs were enhanced with its conductivity. Compared to the control, starch-based film with up to 10 wt% of N-rGO-QDs can still achieve an acceptable optical transparency (60 %–80 %) within 800–500 nm, despite of a quick decline of light transition within 500–300 nm.

Film mechanical property is crucial in its application. Mechanical properties of the films were shown in Fig. 6, and it is that TPS film presented best elastomeric property. Following an addition of N-rGO-QDs from 1.0 wt% to 10.0 wt%, the composite films became stiffer, while the tensile strength and Young's modulus were evidently increased from 16.3 to 54.6 MPa and 354.5 to 467.9 MPa, respectively. As expected, the film with 10.0 wt% N-rGO-QDs showed the most distinct strain-hardening behavior, due to the strong interactions between N-rGO-QDs and starch chains, which had thus caused the interfacial stress. Additionally, the well-dispersed N-rGO-QDs nanoparticles in the starch matrix facilitate a uniform stress distribution. Moreover, the composite film with 3.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs shows excellent durability over the repetitive bending (see movie S1 in the Supporting Information). Obviously, the folding performance of TPS becomes better with an addition of N-rGO-QDs, due to the rigid structure of graphene which enhanced the folding ability of the starch-based film (Fig. 6D). Such intrinsic flexibility is favorable for its applications in some frontier domains, like monitoring human mobile activities.

Film mechanical property is crucial in its application. Mechanical properties of the films were shown in Fig. 6, and it is that TPS film presented best elastomeric property. Following an addition of N-rGO-QDs from 1.0 wt% to 10.0 wt%, the composite films became stiffer, while the tensile strength and Young's modulus were evidently increased from 16.3 to 54.6 MPa and 354.5 to 467.9 MPa, respectively. As expected, the film with 10.0 wt% N-rGO-QDs showed the most distinct strain-hardening behavior, due to the strong interactions between N-rGO-QDs and starch chains, which had thus caused the interfacial stress. Additionally, the well-dispersed N-rGO-QDs nanoparticles in the starch matrix facilitate a uniform stress distribution. Moreover, the composite film with 3.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs shows excellent durability over the repetitive bending (see movie S1 in the Supporting Information). Obviously, the folding performance of TPS becomes better with an addition of N-rGO-QDs, due to the rigid structure of graphene which enhanced the folding ability of the starch-based film (Fig. 6D). Such intrinsic flexibility is favorable for its applications in some frontier domains, like monitoring human mobile activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and conductivity of TPS/N-rGO-QDs film
ce:section.ce:para: N-rGO-QDs showed a strong photoluminescence performance (Fig. 7A) under a UV lamp with 350 nm excitation due to their embedded double bond structural features. The weak shoulder peak that appeared at 290 nm was from the electron transition of π–π* in the molecular orbital of aromatic sp2 hybridized CC domains, while the broad band at ~350 nm was from the n-π* electron transition in the molecular orbital of the functional groups, which was suggested to be induced by the relative transitions of conjugated C=N/C-N, electron-hole recombination or free zig-zag sites (Chen et al., 2018). Compared to native GQDs, N-rGO-QDs exhibited a stronger fluorescence intensity, which is attributed to the more electron holes induced during the process of nitrogen atom doping (Fei et al., 2014). Moreover, N-rGO-QDs showed a strong absorption peak ranged from 250 to 450 nm, suggesting a more efficient electron transition occurred (Li et al., 2018). To achieve optimal fluorescence performance of N-rGO-QDs, a series concentration of N-rGO-QDs was added to obtain the relevant fluorescence intensity. In Fig. 7D, the strongest fluorescence intensity of N-rGO-QDs was obtained when the concentration was 37.5 mg/L, whereas followed by quenching of fluorescence quenching intensity if given a continuous addition of the N-rGO-QDs, which was attributed to the enhancement of Förster resonance energy transfer and quantum size effect, which was caused by aggregation of GQDs (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017). In addition, it is noted that N-rGO-QDs showed a stable fluorescence performance over 20 days (see Fig. S6).

The conductivity mechanism of N-rGO-QDs was investigated by CV using a three-electrode system. Compared to the controls of bare GCE and GQDs/GCE (Fig. 7B-C), N-rGO-QDs/GCE exhibited a higher reduction-oxidation intensity as well as a lower resistance (about 75 Ω), which could be attributed to the lone pair of electrons conjugated to the π-conjugated ring structure (Lee et al., 2013). Therefore, N-rGO-QDs presented an excellent electrocatalytic activity due to the larger surface and pore volume, which accelerated electron transfer in oxidation-reduction reaction, (Fei et al., 2014; Hu & Dai, 2016; Riaz et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2016). In Fig. 7B, it is also observed that N-rGO-QDs had lower band-gap energy with a value of 0.18 eV, strongly indicating an easier electron transfer from the highest occupied molecular orbital energy level (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital took place (LUMO) (Bayat & Saievar, 2017; Fei et al., 2014). As expected, an excellent fluorescent and conductive performance of N-rGO-QDs was achieved.

To investigate the conductivity of these starch-based composite film, the sheet resistance was rationally tuned through controlling the content of N-rGO-QDs. Obviously, an addition of N-rGO-QDs significantly improved the conductivity by reducing the resistance. As shown in Table 1 , square resistance (Rs) and the resistivity of the original TPS film were high to ~106 Ω/sq. and ~ 103 Ω·m, while these two resistance parameters were decreased by 1–2 magnitude orders to ~105/103 Ω/sq. and ~ 105/103 Ω·m respectively even as a result of a slight addition of 1.0–3.0 wt% N-rGO-QDs. Impressively, after an addition of 5.0 wt% N-rGO-QDs, they could be dramatically reduced to the values as low as 558.72 Ω/sq. and 0.196 Ω·m, and such values could be further reduced following a further increase in N-rGO-QDs content. Such evident changes are attributed to the multiple overlapped conductive layers of the N-rGO-QDs which are interlinked through a large-plane π bond and in favor of the electron migration (Li et al., 2018). The strength of quantum size effect often plays a crucial role in modulating the fluorescence and electrical conductivity, since a weaker quantum size effect of GQDs was seen to result in a declined fluorescence and stronger conductivity (Tsysar et al., 2020). In the study, the decrease of film resistance was thereby attributed to the weaker quantum size effect due to an increase of N-rGO-QDs plane size (Li et al., 2017).

The solid-state fluorescence performance of composite films was also investigated by controlling the content of N-rGO-QDs. As clearly shown in Fig. 8A, the solid-state fluorescence intensity of the composite film was weakened significantly with increasing addition of N-rGO-QDs, caused by the aggregation of N-rGO-QDs. In Fig. 8B, no shift in UV–vis absorption peaks of composite films occurred, whereas most broad peak with 5.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs may attribute to the dispersion and aggregate of N-rGO-QDs existing simultaneously. Meanwhile, a broad, low and dual-emission peak at 450 nm and 500 nm was observed in Fig. 8C, which is inferred from the different sizes and morphologies of N-rGO-QDs with weak quantum size effect of some GQDs aggregate in the composite films (Li et al., 2017). The strongest fluorescence intensity of the composite film was achieved when 5.0 wt% of the N-rGO-QDs was applied, showing stable solid-state fluorescence within the testing period of 20 days (Fig. 8D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film
ce:section.ce:para:
As a proof of concept, the synthesized flexible, and folding TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films with the low resistivity of 0.082 Ω·m was utilized as the bending sensors. The TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films exhibited an instant alterable conductivity following the dynamic physical deformation (Fig. 9). When the composite films with 3.0–10.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs were bended from 30 to 90°, an instant increase of ΔR/R
0 (from ~25 % to 2500 %, RSDs<5.7 %) was significantly observed (Fig. 9A-B). Such responsive conductivity grounded on the assumption that physical deformation might be induced by a severe disintegration of the conductive additive with large plane structure (Domene-López et al., 2019; He et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2020). More importantly, the film with 10.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs is more sensitive to bending, which is possibly induced from the disintegrating GQDs aggregate causing a strong quantum size effect of N-rGO-QDs during bending process (Li et al., 2017). Moreover, in Fig. 9C-D, the change for ΔR/R0 during the successive and repeated bending also showed highly reproducible and reversible signal responses (>20 cycles, RSDs<10 %), revealing high reliability for a small-scale sensing of bending, and well demonstrated as a piece of video evidence in movie S2.

As a proof of concept, the synthesized flexible, and folding TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films with the low resistivity of 0.082 Ω·m was utilized as the bending sensors. The TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films exhibited an instant alterable conductivity following the dynamic physical deformation (Fig. 9). When the composite films with 3.0–10.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs were bended from 30 to 90°, an instant increase of ΔR/R
0 (from ~25 % to 2500 %, RSDs<5.7 %) was significantly observed (Fig. 9A-B). Such responsive conductivity grounded on the assumption that physical deformation might be induced by a severe disintegration of the conductive additive with large plane structure (Domene-López et al., 2019; He et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2020). More importantly, the film with 10.0 wt% of N-rGO-QDs is more sensitive to bending, which is possibly induced from the disintegrating GQDs aggregate causing a strong quantum size effect of N-rGO-QDs during bending process (Li et al., 2017). Moreover, in Fig. 9C-D, the change for ΔR/R0 during the successive and repeated bending also showed highly reproducible and reversible signal responses (>20 cycles, RSDs<10 %), revealing high reliability for a small-scale sensing of bending, and well demonstrated as a piece of video evidence in movie S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reliability testing of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the reliability of starch substrate, TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film was tested for its stability via a detailed analysis of the key electrical resistance and resistivity over the long term and under varied humidity (Lu et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S8, the resistance and resistivity values of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film were stable within 10 days of continuous use or exposure under 33–75 % of the RH environments, suggesting its potential durable performance under humid human environments.

TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film was even further subjected to the complete aqueous tests. It was seen in Fig. S9 that multiple repeating certain physical deformation (e.g., bending in a certain angle) of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film could trigger instant, regular and similar electrical signals as feedback in terms of some typical parameters i.e., ΔR/R0, current. All the results indicated a continuous-conductive performance of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film on linking any physical deformation with the output electrical signals, which is thus suitable for wearable electrical devices besides human bodies. Moreover, many works have attempted to improve the water resistance of starch-based materials by chemical modification, blending or coating with hydrophobic materials (Chen et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2019). The next work may consider improving the water resistance of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film.

Whilst TPS/N-rGO-QDs is not secondary to those traditional electrical films by showing above the comparable performance in flexibility, conductivity and fluorescence, starch is also advantageous as a biodegradable, recyclable, and cost-effective substrate. The biodegradable starch substrate is firstly within the scope of green chemistry to maximally reduce the environmental burden. Moreover, there are more hydroxyl groups widespread in starch substrate molecules in favor of the effective interaction with the N-rGO-QDs (Fig. 1a). Therefore, starch-based film was seen able to maintain an ideal stable electronical sensitivity over the five times of recovery via repetitive gelatinization (Fig. S7), which suggests TPS/N-rGO-QDs film can be multiple times recycled to become more cost effective. However, unlike the metal or petrol-chemical substrates (e.g., Au, Ag, PET, PEN, PC, ITO), it is noted that starch molecules as an endogenous highly hydrophilic substrate may be deficient since the inadequate hydrophobic properties (Table S4) would shorten lifetime or undermine functional performances of TPS/N-rGO-QDs film during a close contact with the humid human body environments. To overcome the deficiency, a further chemical modification may be needed to impose a hydrophobic coating onto the surface of present TPS/N-rGO-QDs film in future.

To sum up all the findings above, the hypothesis is well achieved by preparing a novel TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film of excellent performance on flexibility, fluorescence and electroconductivity, and the films are hopeful to be practically employed as a wearable bending sensor to trace the small-scale human movements directly. It is noted the starch-based film composited with 10 wt% N-rGO-QDs showed the lower resistivity (8.4 × 10−2 Ω·m), thinner thickness (~0.22 mm), and more flexible performance. The research novelty lies in that the starch-based film was endowed with multi-functions by compositing with N-rGO-QDs. However, we must note that the TPS/N-rGO-QDs film has some limitations. For example, the synthetic N-rGO-QDs may cause potential risks to human health (Bottrill & Green, 2011; Hardman, 2005), poor hydrophobicity, weak elongation and the addition of N-rGO-QDs might cause an unavoidable interruption on the uniformity of the starch-based film, leading to some unfavorable performances. To address the challenge, a further cytotoxicity assay is needed to evaluate the toxicity of N-rGO-QDs. In addition, another limitation lies in the possible inadequate distribution of N-rGO-QDs all over the film, which needs to be improved via more pretreatments (e.g., sonication).

The novelty of the research lies in the preparation of a new TPS/N-rGO-QDs film that has shown more excellent performance on flexibility, fluorescence and electroconductivity than the previous flexible conductive/solid-state fluorescence materials. The hypothesis is achieved that the synthesized starch-based composite film showed high electronic conductivity (resistivity is 0.082 Ω·m), excellent angle-bending sensitivity (ΔR/R0 is from ~25 % to 2500 %, RSDs<5.7 %), and durable fluorescence stability (>20 days). Despite the current deficiencies in weakening fluorescence/optical transmittance or declining breaking elongation due to the addition of the functional N-rGO-QDs particles, the novel starch-based composite film after a further improvement is of the potential value to be applied as components in the wearable photoelectric sensors.

ce:def-description.ce:para: nitrogen-doped oxide reduced graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Movie S1
Folding test of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite film.
Movie S1
Movie S2
The relative resistance varied over physical bending of TPS/N-rGO-QDs composite films under real-time monitoring.
Movie S2
The supplementary data of harnessing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for enhancing the fluorescence and conductivity of the starch-based film.
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.120475.

Chenxi Liu: Data acquisition and analysis, Writing–original draft. Mengying Wen: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Shihua Mai: Methodology, Software. Yue Ma: Methodology, Software. Qingfei Duan: Material characterization. Xianyang Bao: Characterization. Wei Zou: Conceptualization, design of the work. Hongsheng Liu: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization, writing–review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge research funds Natural Science Foundation of China (32272340, 22178124), the 111 project (B17018), Science and technology plan project of Guangzhou (202102080150). National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of South China University of China.
